title: Local Correctability of Expander Codes
speaker: Brett Hemenway
abstract: An error-correcting code is called locally decodable if there exists a decoding algorithm that can recover any symbol of the message with high probability by reading only a small number of symbols of the corrupted codeword. There is a fundamental tradeoff in locally decodable codes between the rate (the ratio of message length to codeword length) and the locality (the number of symbols read by the decoder). Ideally, one strives for high rate and low locality.

proof_stages.label: Local-correctability model and target parameters
proof_stages.summary: The talk fixes the randomized worst-case definition of local correctability, relates it to local decodability for linear systematic codes, and identifies the goal of rate approaching one with sublinear locality.

proof_stages.label: Tanner expander-code setup and rate accounting
proof_stages.summary: A Tanner code is built by placing symbols on graph edges and requiring the incident symbols at each vertex to lie in a length-d inner code, with a parity-check construction giving the basic rate lower bound 2r0-1 and motivating expander graphs for efficient decoding.

proof_stages.label: Reduction to inner smooth reconstruction
proof_stages.summary: The main theorem is framed as a reduction: a Tanner code on a d-regular expander becomes locally correctable when the inner code admits q-query smooth reconstruction, meaning noiseless recovery of any coordinate from uniformly distributed queries.

proof_stages.label: Error-free random-walk reconstruction tree
proof_stages.summary: Iterating the inner smooth reconstructor unfolds a q-ary tree of random walks in the expander whose leaves would determine the target edge in the absence of errors, and logarithmic-depth mixing yields the n^epsilon locality exponent from q versus d.

proof_stages.label: Second tree and path-divergence principle
proof_stages.summary: To handle corrupted leaves, each mixed leaf is corrected by reading a full second reconstruction tree, using the fact that two valid trees with different root symbols must differ along an entire root-to-leaf path.

proof_stages.label: Bottom-up path-error decoder
proof_stages.summary: The decoder processes a read tree from the leaves upward, computing for each possible root value the shortest corrupted path length needed to realize it, and selects the value requiring the fewest path errors.

proof_stages.label: Expander-walk concentration against adversarial errors
proof_stages.summary: Because the second trees are rooted after sufficient random-walk mixing, the adversarial error set is seen almost like a random sparse set, so expander-walk bounds make a heavily corrupted path exponentially unlikely and the green leaves are recovered accurately.

proof_stages.label: Affine-geometry/Ramanujan instantiation and parameter tradeoff
proof_stages.summary: Using affine-geometry inner codes for smooth reconstruction and Ramanujan graphs for expansion gives codes of rate 1-alpha, locality n^epsilon, and constant error tolerance, while the closing discussion explains that improving locality would require better control of the Tanner-code rate for lower-rate inner codes.

examples.name: Identity code for an average-index notion
examples.note: In response to a question about randomness, the identity code illustrates why the definition must require success for every index and corruption pattern rather than only on average.

examples.name: Matching-vector constant-query codes

examples.name: Tanner's K_{7,7} Hamming-code ordering comparison
examples.note: This historical calculation illustrates that different edge orderings can give widely different rate-distance parameters and that the crude constraint-counting rate bound is weak.
examples.engagement_reason: he compares Tanner’s K_{7,7} Hamming-ordering examples by listing the resulting [49,k,d] parameters and checking the trivial rate bound 2·(4/7)−1=1/7
